skill.name: aiops
skill.description: Framework-agnostic AIOps guidance for intelligent monitoring, incident response, predictive operations, self-healing automation, and observability across cloud and Kubernetes environments.
skill.summary: Plan and implement AIOps monitoring, automation, incident response, and observability patterns.
skill.author: Azeem-2

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-crypto alerts are mostly false positives from Markdown code fences and ordinary text. The skill is legitimate AIOps guidance, but copied examples include Kubernetes-changing automation, outbound API calls, credential-bearing Datadog headers, and user-provided webhook delivery, so publication should include an operational-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Infrastructure-Changing Automation Examples
medium_findings.description: The automation engine examples construct kubectl scale, restart, patch, isolate, and failback commands from action targets and parameters, then execute them with asyncio.create_subprocess_exec. This is legitimate AIOps behavior, but copied code could change production infrastructure if used without authorization checks, input validation, and change controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command construction and subprocess execution are directly visible, and the handlers modify Kubernetes resources. The risk is operational misuse rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Outbound Integrations Can Carry Operational Data
medium_findings.description: The examples create HTTP sessions for monitoring systems and post notifications to a webhook URL supplied in action parameters. This is expected for AIOps integrations, but unvalidated endpoints can expose alert messages, service names, and operational metadata.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network calls and user-supplied webhook posting are explicit in the examples. No evidence shows malicious exfiltration, but the data-flow risk is real if implemented without allowlists.

low_findings.title: Credential Parameters Used for Datadog API Headers
low_findings.description: The Datadog integration accepts API and application keys and places them into request headers. This is a normal integration pattern, but implementations should load credentials from a secrets manager and avoid logging or hardcoding them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly handles API keys, but the keys are constructor parameters rather than embedded secrets. The finding is a handling concern, not evidence of credential theft.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Weak-Crypto Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak-cryptography locations point to prose, dataclass fields, feature extraction, dashboard descriptions, and closing text. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA-1, or another weak cryptographic algorithm at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted line review shows ordinary descriptive text and field names, not cryptographic calls. This strongly supports a false-positive classification.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence Command Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Several Ruby backtick execution findings correspond to Markdown code fences that delimit Python examples. They are documentation formatting, not executable Ruby shell backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are Markdown delimiters around code blocks. They do not create shell execution behavior inside the skill file itself.

low_findings.title: Identifier-Based Reconnaissance Alerts Are Low Risk
low_findings.description: The system-reconnaissance matches identify generated execution and healing IDs based on action, target, service name, and timestamp. These identifiers can reveal service names in logs, but they do not enumerate host or environment details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines create IDs from operational names and timestamps. The privacy concern is plausible but limited because no host discovery or broad enumeration is shown.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess-Based Kubernetes Remediation
dangerous_patterns.description: The example remediation engine executes kubectl commands from action definitions. Use strict RBAC, allowlists, dry-run support, and human approval before adapting this pattern to production.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call and Kubernetes command assembly are explicit. The code avoids shell=True, but the commands can still cause infrastructure changes.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Provided Webhook Delivery
dangerous_patterns.description: The notification handler posts alert content to a webhook URL from action parameters. Implementations should restrict destinations and redact sensitive incident details.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The outbound POST target comes from parameters and sends operational message content. This is a common integration feature, but it needs endpoint controls.

content.value_statement: Modern operations teams need faster incident response and clearer infrastructure signals. This skill provides AIOps patterns for monitoring, automation, prediction, and observability planning.
actual_capabilities: Explains AIOps platform architecture using alerts, metrics, logs, traces, and incidents.
Shows integration patterns for Prometheus, Loki, Jaeger, and Datadog data sources.
Provides anomaly detection, pattern analysis, and predictive operations examples.
Outlines automation and self-healing workflows for Kubernetes-based remediation.
Covers observability dashboards, SLO tracking, and operational recommendations.
limitations: It is guidance and sample code, not a packaged production AIOps platform.
Automation examples require security review before use in live environments.
Provider integrations may need updates for current API behavior and authentication rules.
It does not replace incident response process design or change approval controls.

use_cases.title: Design an AIOps Platform
use_cases.description: Plan the core data models, integrations, and workflows for an AI-assisted operations platform.

use_cases.title: Improve Incident Response
use_cases.description: Draft alert enrichment, action plans, and safe remediation workflows for recurring incidents.

use_cases.title: Evaluate Observability Maturity
use_cases.description: Compare existing monitoring, tracing, dashboard, and SLO practices against AIOps patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Map Current Signals
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the AIOps skill to map our current metrics, logs, traces, alerts, and incident workflows. Identify missing signals and first improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Monitoring Integrations
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the AIOps skill to design integrations for Prometheus, Loki, Jaeger, and Datadog. Include authentication, data flow, and failure handling.

prompt_templates.title: Design Safe Remediation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the AIOps skill to design self-healing actions for Kubernetes services. Include approvals, RBAC, dry-run checks, rollback, and audit logging.

prompt_templates.title: Build an AIOps Roadmap
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the AIOps skill to create a phased roadmap for anomaly detection, incident automation, SLO management, and predictive maintenance across three quarters.

output_examples.input: Assess our monitoring stack for AIOps readiness.
output: A readiness summary grouped by metrics, logs, traces, alerts, and incident data.
A prioritized gap list covering data quality, ownership, and alert enrichment.
A phased recommendation plan for integration and automation work.

output_examples.input: Create a self-healing plan for a failing Kubernetes service.
output: A remediation workflow with detection criteria, safe action limits, and approval gates.
A rollback and escalation path for failed automated actions.
Security controls for RBAC, input validation, and audit logging.

output_examples.input: Define SLO reporting for a customer-facing API.
output: Candidate availability and latency objectives tied to user impact.
Recommended burn-rate views and dashboard panels.
Incident response triggers based on error budget consumption.

best_practices: Treat all remediation examples as design patterns until reviewed for your environment.
Use least-privilege service accounts, destination allowlists, and secret management for integrations.
Start with observability quality before adding predictive models or automated repair actions.
anti_patterns: Do not run automated patch, restart, or isolation actions without approval controls.
Do not send incident details to arbitrary webhooks or unapproved external services.
Do not assume sample API integrations are production-ready without provider-specific validation.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps design AIOps systems for monitoring, alerting, incident response, automation, prediction, and observability.

faq.question: Does it install or run an AIOps platform?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation patterns and examples that teams can adapt after review.

faq.question: Which tools are referenced?
faq.answer: The examples reference Prometheus, Loki, Jaeger, Datadog, Kubernetes, Slack-style webhooks, and common Python data tools.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: Is the automation safe for production?
faq.answer: Not by default. Production use requires RBAC, validation, approval gates, dry runs, rollback, and audit logging.

faq.question: What should teams review first?
faq.answer: Review secret handling, outbound integrations, Kubernetes permissions, alert data sensitivity, and change management requirements.
